role_summary: Co-founder and captain of the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party, 1968-1972.
ai_reading: On the concept axis the corpus calls Nonviolence as Theology vs Armed Self-Defense (raw position values run from -1 at the Nonviolence pole to +1 at the Self-Defense pole), Aaron Dixon's testimony sits at -0.73, far toward the Nonviolence as Theology end and among the strongest pulls in that direction across the entire 136-interview corpus. That is the opposite of where his cultural placement predicts: Black Panther captains are read in historiography as armed-self-defense voices, not theological-nonviolence ones. The four interviewees closer to the Nonviolence as Theology pole than Dixon are his brother Elmer (entry 37, at -1.00), Elbert "Big Man" Howard (BPP co-founder, entry 35, at -0.85), the joint Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson interview (at -0.85), and Kathleen Cleaver (BPP, entry 73, at -0.80); three of those four are Black Panther-identified, exactly the figures whose political reception would predict the opposite pole. Whether the embedding is catching something real about BPP oral testimony reading as more redemptive and community-service-oriented than the party's rhetorical surface (Dixon's Free Breakfast for Children program and his Seattle medical-and-legal clinic are documented in the orientation paragraph below), or a polarity convention in the axis-vector definition that reverses the political mapping, is a question this catalog leaves open for the paper-in-progress that treats these AI-vs-culture mismatches as its central data.
biographical_paragraph: The Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party formed in 1968 with Aaron Dixon as its founding captain, the first such chapter to organize beyond California [src: 2]. Dixon was nineteen when he took the post, having been born in Chicago on January 2, 1949 [src: 2]. Under his captaincy through 1972 the Seattle chapter ran a Free Breakfast for Children program and opened a community health and legal services clinic still in operation today as the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center [src: 2]. The same year the chapter formed, Dixon traveled to Oakland for Bobby Hutton's funeral and met Kathleen Cleaver there for the first time; Cleaver is also represented in this archive, at entry 73 [src: 2]. Four decades later, in 2012, Dixon returned to that organizing arc in book form with the Haymarket Books memoir titled My People Are Rising: Memoir of a Black Panther Party Captain [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history sits at entry 1 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].

sources.title: Aaron Dixon oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
